name: Miller–Rabin Primality Test
description: Stronger than Fermat: write n−1 = 2^r·d (d odd). For each witness a, check a^d mod n and a^(2^j·d) for j=0..r−1. If a^d ≡ ±1 or some a^(2^j·d) ≡ −1, n is strong probable prime. Else composite. No Carmichael failures; error prob ≤ 4^−k for k rounds. O(k log³ n). Used in practice (OpenSSL, etc.).
mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Input: n, rounds k"]
    B["n even/small? → composite/trivial"]
    C["Write n−1 = 2^r · d"]
    D["Pick witness a"]
    E["Compute a^d, a^2d, ..., a^n−1 mod n"]
    F{"Strong pseudoprime\ncondition?"}
    G["Composite"]
    H["Probably prime"]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F -->|No| G
    F -->|Yes| H
    classDef red fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    classDef green fill:#51cf66,color:#fff
    classDef lavender fill:#e6e6fa,color:#333
    class A red
    class B,C,D,E,H green
    class F lavender
    class G red
